data.title: Richtech Robotics: I've Never Wanted To Give A Sell Rating So Badly (Downgrade)
data.description:
I have a couple of regrets in my life, but only a few rival the anguish I've felt about the fact that I gave Richtech Robotics (NASDAQ:RR) a hold rating when I covered it back in December. My conclusion at the time was that this was a speculative endeavor that isn't likely to work out, but that such is the nature of most “innovation co.”-style firms that bleed money on cutting-edge tech. My conclusion of “maybe moonshot” was reasonable for the information I had found at the time.

I've learned some things since then that changed my mind dramatically, and I'm downgrading the stock as much as possible. Truthfully, this isn't me delivering breaking news; the market figured a lot of this out before me.

Here's the scorecard so you can see how bad it's been for them since I wrote the hold article in December:

Spending More, Making Less

RR is a robotics manufacturer based in Las Vegas, NV, that produces several models of robots. They are designed to perform tasks normally assigned to low-skill workers, like in warehousing, serving, and very basic manufacturing (demos show the robots putting screws into metal cases).

These are not a profitable offering in our current market; the company consistently loses money, with its last quarterly loss its highest on record, at ($8.41M).

I always want to give these kinds of firms the benefit of the doubt, because I understand the “you have to spend money to make money” mantra, and so unprofitability isn't always a hard pass.

But what are they spending on? Breaking down their spending, we see that their last quarterly spend on their cost of goods was $0.90M, their spending on research and development was at $0.34M, and their capital expenditures were at $0.07M. This looks like a lean operation when you remove the overhead. Overhead expenses cost Richtech $11.96M last quarter. The chart shows how truly divergent these costs are.

Okay, that's not a lot of R&D or CapEx, so we're not spending so much on making better (or more) robots or facilities to make better robots. Although CapEx spikes in the past suggest that's been undertaken before—they can reach as high as $5M in a given quarter—even if the nominal figures feel very low for a robotics manufacturer compared to how much they spend on overhead.

I could almost forgive this huge overhead expense if it's in the name of getting sales going. The CapEx spend looks to be ramping down, R&D spending is flat, and so that means we have to be trying to sell and deploy as many robots as possible, right?

No, actually—the sales and marketing budget is flat too, sitting at $0.19M. The rest is all going to administrative costs. G&A costs are notoriously sticky, as it's much easier to raise than to lower salaries and other costs like rent for facilities, counsel, advisory fees, etc. So when a firm spends so dramatically on this expense, it should be a warning sign to investors. The $11.77M spent on G&A last quarter likely did very little to further the products or Richtech's sales, and so it is unlikely to see a high ROI in the end.

At this point, I shouldn't have to ask this question, but we need to know to come to a data-driven conclusion about this expense. Is it generating more sales? After all, who am I to judge Richtech's spending if it produces results? Motivated executives can be mighty assets for a plucky business in this space.

Revenue hasn't meaningfully moved over the last two years; the latest spike looks similar to July 2024, before the 5x increase in G&A spending, so I don't think we can call any victories here. The last quarterly read came in below the 2-year average; the average sits at $1.16M, and last quarter, Richtech brought in $1.15M in revenue. The needle isn't moving much despite the increased spending.

To raise the money to pay for all of these expenses, since revenue isn't an option, Richtech has had to routinely issue new shares. This dilution is problematic because it can water down returns for shareholders even if the stock does make genuine gains.

The big problem: issuance is accelerating. Richtech tends to issue new shares periodically but irregularly, without much investor notice beforehand. These announcements tend to lead to sudden sell-offs in the stock, though they also tend to be temporary reactions.

The diluted weighted average shares outstanding on the income statement show an increase from 70M in September 2024 to 122M as of September 2025, a ~74% increase.

The total shares outstanding, which includes those taken out of the diluted figure, have gone from ~61M in 2023 at the IPO to 223.6M three years later, as more shares have been issued since the last annual statement.

New Deals Don't Make Me Bullish; The Old Deals Were Bearish

But perhaps I am moving too fast here. New announcements are being made for deployment across Europe. NewConsultancy, an IT services and consulting firm with a single office in the Netherlands, would be the touchpoint for the EU single market to buy Richtech robots from. That would give Richtech access to the full Eurozone and Schengen Area once their robots have been imported by NewConsultancy, although it's unclear how many units will be sent or demanded by the market there. No details were given in the official press releases by either organization, although Richtech robots are already featured on the company's public-facing website. The market seems to believe it represents an opportunity to meaningfully move the revenue figure, and I agree, even if I'm skeptical about the execution.

From what I can tell, Richtech has a history of being overly optimistic about their potential deployments. While no numbers were given for this latest announcement in Europe, they had previously announced a deployment of 500 robotic arms in collaboration with Park34 Liquor Store Inc. over 5 years and then the deployment of 500 robots per year thereafter.

This was announced as a distributor's agreement in September 2024, but as of today, I cannot find any robots listed for sale, mentioned, or featured on Park34 Liquor's website. The business in question operates a single liquor store and tasting room in Manhattan but does appear to do distribution business to several states, from what I can find online. Perhaps they only do business in person with local NYC businesses to this end. I am unsure. There have been no updates from Richtech since that announcement regarding progress on this deal.

History tells us that this new partnership may be similarly over-optimistically estimated as well and may not pan out as expected, and so the stock gains from that day may be unwarranted over the long run. Just as past partnership announcements have seen their gains given up over the following weeks, I would expect the same here.

The Products Make Me The Most Bearish

For context, here's the coverage of their products from my last article, when I was more naive and generous than I am now:

The flagship robot that has already been deployed is a drink mixer, more or less. ADAM is the "AI-powered bartender, barista, and boba maker that wows your customers and boosts your bottom line," according to RR's website. I am not all too impressed with these kinds of robots, mostly because I see these kinds of robots as showpieces and not real game-changers for businesses.

Maybe I'm wrong, but the TAM for people who want a robot bartender to serve them drinks (more than just at a one-time event where it's a novelty) is fairly small compared to industrial needs.

Here's where things get interesting for me, the industrial bots.

The company's newest humanoid robot is called Dex, and it is supposed to offer firms a whole different kind of automation experience. It was debuted in late October this year, and has not seen any real deployment as far as I can tell. It's likely too soon to have fulfilled orders, to be fair.

They boast that Dex "fits into the systems you already use." This is the major selling point for many manufacturers and industrial firms that have struggled to use legacy automation tools and robots, because they were typically too bulky and too hyper-specialized (or else they were not cost-effective) to plug straight into a business. Entire factories needed to be refitted and rebuilt, causing time delays and imposing costs that many firms simply refused to shoulder.

Humanoid robots like Dex promise to fix this dilemma, which is the largest selling point for them.

Deployment has been slow since then, with little news. Here's where we stood back in December on deployments:

...There is a BMW (OTC:BMWKY) dealership in Plano, Texas that they have deployed Titan at. RR has a promotional video on the deployment. They call it a "case study" on their website, but it's 2 minutes long, so I refuse to repeat that in any seriousness.

Otherwise, it seems like most of the deployments so far have been to one-time corporate events. While good for exposure and investor interest, RR has been unable to materially affect its bottom line yet with robotics deployments.

That's expected, since the company is in its early stages, but it is definitely something to consider when investing now. We are not at the point yet where they can claim large-scale success in deployment.

Since then, Richtech has announced a collaboration with Microsoft (MSFT) to use Azure AI in Richtech's robots. This news was a boon for the stock, but it's unclear how much of a difference it will really make for their software deployment since it's unclear which robots will be given Azure AI software updates.

Most worryingly in the product realm, the competition from China is fierce. Here is a very similar model to Richtech's from a Chinese manufacturer, OrionStar, a ~72%-owned subsidiary of Cheetah Mobile (CMCM), that was released two years before ADAM.

Richtech buys materials from them indirectly through another subsidiary called UFactory, which Cheetah Mobile bought ~80% of in July.

Looking through other products, Richtech's Titan bot, the one deployed at the BMW dealership in Plano, looks incredibly similar to the AutoXing Industrial Robot.

The specs are very similar too, although it's tough to find the exact 1:1 comparisons because not every spec is included in the product sheets of both models. I included the specs as they were and added conversions for context in parentheses:

Richtech Titan AutoXing Industrial Robot
Lower Lift 330 lb (150 kg) 150 kg (330 lb)
Higher Lift 1,300 lb (600 kg) 600 kg (1,300 lb)
Battery Life 10-24 hr ~12 hr
Speed 3.9 ft/s (1.2 m/s) 1.2 m/s (3.9 ft/s)
Operating System Android & Linux Android & Linux
Sensors LiDAR, RGB LiDAR, RBG
Navigation Free/Hybrid/Track Free/Hybrid/Track
Connectivity 4G/Wifi 4G/Wifi

Note: Richtech claims on its Titan spec page that some models can lift 1,200 lb, but marketing on the same webpage also claims that 330 lb is the maximum. I am inclined to believe the spec sheet, and that the 330 comment refers to the base model Titan, which is in line with AutoXing's base model as well.

Some of these are just the manufacturers following industrial standards, but it's hard not to notice how little the Richtech variant stands out. Its battery life appears to be better, but perhaps it's just because it's presented as a range rather than a middle point.

The same goes for essentially all the models of robots that Richtech manufactures, including the Dust-E line. The three models that Richtech produces all have competitors produced by Sparkoz.

Here are their specs side-by-side for a similar comparison:

Richtech Dust-E MX Sparkoz TN70-Pro
Dimensions 44"x22"x45" 46"x23"x48"
Climbing Grade 6° 6°
Dry Cleaning Width 20" 20"
Wet Cleaning Width 30" 32"
Battery Life 3-4 hours ~3.6 hours
Sensors IMU, LiDAR, ultrasonic IMU, LiDAR, ultrasonic
Tank Volume 17 gal 18.5 gal

There seems to be little differentiating the Richtech model from more established competitors with existing distribution deals. It does not seem to me like much innovation has been done to make these models superior, at least not technically.

Before the Matradee L was shelved for the new Matradee Plus, it even carried the same color scheme as its OrionStar counterpart—or perhaps the OrionStar counterpart carries the same color scheme as it. It's unclear who is whose “inspiration” in all of these designs. The new model shares very similar characteristics with the AutoXing Mars Smartbot.

When discussing their R&D spend, Richtech previously justified their spending with the claim that their “Universal Smart Mobile Platform (USMP)” accelerated the development of new robots and that it gives them a distinct competitive edge. But the model of robot that they are using in that example, pictured in the linked disclosure, also has a direct Chinese counterpart that has been on the market at least as long and offers a developer kit for firms to create their own universal mobile platforms. Richtech's software is proprietary, and it's unclear how well it performed because the robot is no longer listed on the Richtech website.

Giving Richtech the benefit of the doubt and making the assumption that their software is superior even if the physical and mechanical robotics are too similar to have much marginal performance improvement over their counterparts, and you still don't come out with a firm that looks like a winner.

Conclusion

Ultimately, Richtech Robotics is building robots that are too similar to existing competitor models out of China. To justify burning as much cash as they do, their products need to deeply impress. But their existing products do not seem innovative, and deployment has been too sparse to get real-world comparisons and case studies. Their ties to Chinese robotics manufacturers that have been making strikingly similar robots in form and function are a liability.

To be willing to invest in an “innovation co.” that loses money in the hopes of making a big breakthrough in a cutting-edge technology, one has to have confidence in the technology. Based on what I've found and seen, I do not have confidence in Richtech Robotics, their products, or any real edge they may have in the market. Even if they do, the rate of dilution is so high that shareholders who are right may have most of their gains eroded by the accelerating issuance of new stock.

The outlook for the stock is still grim despite the recent drawdown, as the path to profitability gets further and further away each quarter; G&A expenses now sit at >5x R&D and CapEx combined, which does not instill confidence that this company is ready for a turnaround where they export robots across Europe. This new deal has given the market some confidence in RR, but I believe it is ill-placed. Richtech has rightfully earned a strong sell rating from me, and I would recommend avoiding this stock at all costs.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Radware: Not Enough Differentiation
data.description:
Introduction

I wanted to take a look at another undercovered company on Seeking Alpha to see if there may be a hidden gem opportunity. The company in question is Radware Ltd. (RDWR). Since it just reported its full-year 2025 performance, there is no better time to delve deeper than now.

The Company

Radware provides protection for applications, APIs, and network infrastructure, with the focus currently on cloud security through recurring revenue. As with many such companies, Radware is switching from product-like licensing to cloud services, subscriptions, and maintenance. The favorite metric the management likes to focus on, as well as analysts because of it, is annual recurring revenue, or ARR.

Radware’s application helps protect against DDoS attacks, web application firewalls, bot management, and API security.

For differentiation, the company leans into specialized application protection and DDoS defense rather than a broad security platform, with the use of machine-learning-based mitigation through behavioral baselines.

Performance in 2025

Products grew at around 22% y/y to $189.6m, while services declined around 6% to $112.3m. Overall, total revenue grew close to 10% for the year.

We can see that the growth engine for 2025 was the product segment. Why is that? The services revenue declined because Radware kept shifting toward subscription and cloud delivery and because the company saw double-digit growth in defense products.

The decline in services isn’t worrying me. I don’t see it as a weakness, as it is just a mix shift toward higher-value recurring offerings, which I am confident will recover in the future.

Taking a look at the company’s profitability, we can see something quite unusual. Gross margins are fantastic; however, essentially none of the gross profit trickles down into the income statement. It seems that the company spends particularly heavily on sales and marketing, as well as research and development. Sales and marketing alone accounted for 42% of total revenues, with R&D coming in at 26%.

Maybe it is because of the nature of the industry, which doesn’t allow for higher profitability. If we look at some of its peers, we can see some of them have much higher operating margins, while another is close to Radware’s, so I believe there is room for improvement.

Given its low bottom line and the nature of the industry, I am not surprised to see that the company’s pricing power is very low. Even AKAM’s return on total capital is below 4%, which tells me 10% is not realistic, but around 5% is more reasonable, as RADCOM (RDCM) is able to achieve.

On the financial position front, Radware finished the year with around $105m in cash and equivalents, as well as $136.2m in short-term deposits. This gives the company a decent $241m cash cushion against no debt, apart from some operating leases. This is a very strong position to be in, which will allow the company to use all the resources available to continue to push its differentiation and gain market share. The company’s free cash flow did decline this year, going from around $66m to $41.5m. Not alarming, but worth keeping an eye on.

Overall, it looks like the company is headed in the right direction. The company’s profitability improved to the point that it is actually profitable on a GAAP basis. The company has no debt on its books and is swimming in cash, which I would like to see being used for further growth of the company.

I don’t particularly like it when companies hoard cash unnecessarily. It seems inefficient, but it may also be the nature of this industry. Cybersecurity companies tend to keep large cash balances for resilience, acquisitions, and some downside protection when things go awry. The company is doing some share buybacks, but I think it could easily do more in this regard, especially since the company’s shareholders aren’t getting much in terms of stock appreciation recently.

Comments on the Outlook

What is the company bringing to the table?

I think this is the most important question to ask when buying any of these cybersecurity companies. Radware is not reinventing what a cybersecurity company is. A lot of its functions overlap with the giants like Cloudflare (NET), Akamai (AKAM), F5 (FFIV), and many others. Others that may have deeper pockets and already established operations with much higher profitability, like AKAM, which I mentioned earlier, boast operating margins close to 15%.

So, can we quantify the growth, and can we say that it is much more attractive to own a smaller company because it can get us higher stock returns?

The management says that growth for 2026 is going to come from mainly three places: cloud ARR, product subscriptions, and new security modules around API security and agentic AI protection. Going forward, the management said that Cloud ARR is the primary catalyst for growth, with total ARR and bookings also helping visibility for 2026. Radware is targeting 25% cloud ARR growth going forward. That is quite a hefty goal, but it may be achievable since the company is still quite low in terms of total revenue for cloud ARR. For 2025, it was up 23% to $95.2m.

Agentic AI protection

I think another big opportunity for the company, especially if they can mitigate the threat very effectively, is protecting AI agents from injection attacks. Prompt injections are a very common way that hackers can talk to the model directly, which is called a direct prompt injection, and another one is called an indirect prompt injection, which is the more dangerous one because there can be malicious instructions hidden inside third-party content. This is bad because if the AI agent has access to your computer, and I’m talking full access, it can read emails, files, query the system, or trigger workflows; then a successful prompt injection can turn into a real operational incident. It can leak company secrets, move data, and execute instructions as a trusted employee. If the company can excel in this type of threat, I can see it becoming a real revenue growth driver.

The management said that if the cloud ARR grows at a 25% CAGR, then by 2030, Radware will be a $500m revenue business. That is quite the goal. I am interested in seeing how it pans out.

Valuation

Let’s look at a what-if situation in terms of modelling the company’s intrinsic value with some estimates. Below are the assumptions I used, which I think are quite optimistic.

I went with 11% 5-year CAGR because I wanted to get to around $500m in revenues by 2030, as the management expects the company to achieve by then. For margins, I see that analysts are expecting $1.11 in EPS for next year, which translates to around 19%, which is the company’s adjusted EBITDA. From then on, I assume further profitability improvements. I don’t think they are too optimistic, since the company’s pricing mix is moving towards higher-margin products and services. For the rest of the model, I am keeping the figures close to what they are at the end of 2025 for simplicity. So, with these assumptions, we get the following projections.

As you can see, by 2030, Radware will have reached over $500m in revenue. And with these projections, we see that the company is worth $21.69 per share, meaning it is currently trading a little over its fair value.

Closing Comments

So far, I am not seeing anything that would make me add the company to my portfolio. There isn’t a lot of growth in the top line and profitability to justify its current share price, and the products that it offers are not very different from those of many other cybersecurity companies out there.

I am interested in seeing how the story pans out over the next few quarters, so I will be adding it to my watchlist so I can follow along, especially what comes out of agentic AI protection, as that is going to be very important in the future as more and more companies create their own agents.

data.title: ASML: GDP Fears Are Rising, But The Technology Edge Isn't Cracking
data.description:
ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) shares declined just like the broader markets following the war in the Middle East. The stock price, however, is recovering and now trades 4% below its 52-week high and has increased 8% since my last report, in which I discussed potential war impacts on ASML. The company will be reporting its first-quarter earnings on the 15th of April. In this report, I discuss how earnings and revenue estimates have developed and update my assessment of the stock price target accordingly.

No Major Revisions For ASML Following Middle East Turmoil

For the first quarter, analysts are expecting ASML to report between $9.48 billion and $10.43 billion, with a consensus estimate of $10.13 billion indicating nearly 15% year-on-year growth. EPS is expected to be between $7.57 and $8.03, with a consensus of $7.77, indicating 13.7% year-on-year growth.

Over the past month, estimates for the first quarter have increased 2.3% for revenues and 2% for earnings per share. Over a three-month period, the estimates on revenues were lowered by 2% on revenues and 2.3% on earnings per share, but over a six-month period, the estimates are still up 10.8% and 19.4%. So, the turmoil in the Middle East does not seem to have caused major downward revision activity. Perhaps that is also not completely unexpected given that freight and logistics costs are a small piece of the pie and ASML’s business nature is that customers place orders for the long term and near-term revenues and earnings are not immediately affected when events such as wars occur.

For ASML, I have looked at the pre-war estimates and the current estimates, and we see that there is no major downward revision. By quarter, the estimates have mostly been lowered for the first quarter, but it is a small downward revision. Estimates from Q2 until Q4 have also been lowered, but not materially. For the full year, the quarterly estimate revisions show a 0.3% downward revision on revenues of 0.2% and 0.5% on EPS. If we look at the annual analyst estimates, we see a downward revision of 0.3% on revenues and 0.2% on EPS. In the years after, estimates have increased by 0.8% and 0.5% on revenues and by 1.5% and 3.8% on EPS. Those are not any major revisions that point at analysts expecting a potential impact on GDP affecting ASML’s business.

What I Will Be Watching For In ASML's Call

For ASML, what I will be watching is mostly the longer-term color they can provide on whether they are seeing any GDP erosion trickling down to their business and whether they are seeing any shifts in AI as the market is growing increasingly concerned about the monetization of AI and AI infrastructure costs. I am also hoping to see the company providing comments on what role the company sees for itself in the packaging part of semiconductor manufacturing. The company shipped the TWINSCAN XT:260 in 2025, focused on advanced semiconductor packaging. Packaging is one of the bottlenecks sending AI infrastructure costs soaring right now. What I would like to hear about is whether the company sees packaging equipment as a growth opportunity that could meaningfully add to the business. With typical lead times of 1.5-3 years, the backlog is going to be a meaningful indicator of demand and growth potential, and as always, that will be a watch item.

ASML Remains A Buy Even On Global Chip Demand

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For ASML, I am increasing my price target by around 3% to $1,643.21, implying 11% upside. We note that EBITDA estimates have increased by 7.2% for 2026-2028, with free cash flow estimates having increased 6%. This includes a 50 basis point increase driven by currency exchange rates. The revisions are backloaded, but we note that EBITDA estimates for this year improved by 0.9%, and free cash flow estimates were largely unchanged but probably are somewhat lower on a EUR-denominated basis due to higher capital expenditures.

Margins are expected to continue improving this year, but we note that it seems to be a less steep improvement compared to the improvement from 2024 to 2025. This is driven by the fact that ASML continues investing in new technology, and when that new technology enters into the top line, it does so at lower initial margins. Free cash flow is set to dip this year for similar reasons, along with an increase in capital expenditures. That it is not a bad thing, as the investments today are required to pave tomorrow’s growth. The company remains in a net cash position, which also provides support for additional share repurchases than currently modeled.

Conclusion: ASML Remains Attractive

ASML has not seen negative revisions from the war in Iran. Whether that remains the same when keeping in mind any GDP impact remains to be seen, but for now it seems that the AI growth cycle is strong enough to offset GDP headwinds. ASML is essentially the cradle of modern semiconductor manufacturing, and I believe that it remains an attractive stock to buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Perimeter Solutions: Appealing Business, Complicated Finances
data.description:
Shares of Perimeter Solutions, Inc. (PRM) have fallen by high single digits since I covered shares late in January. At the time, I concluded that the fire play was on fire, after a strong 2025 and a larger $685 million deal announced for Medical Manufacturing Technologies.

This deal was followed by the release of the full-year results in February as the company ended the year on a solid note. This is encouraging, along with upbeat remarks about the performance of its recent acquisition. Such news is to be applauded amidst a small setback in the share price, making me upbeat on the business.

However, the issue is that of a very complicated founders agreement, resulting in real dilution and cash payouts, seen for years to come and difficult to model, making the situation too complicated to get involved.

Ending 2025 On A Strong Note

In February, Perimeter announced a 19% increase in fourth-quarter sales to nearly $103 million in what seasonally is a softer quarter for the year. This made full-year sales rise by 16% to nearly $653 million. Growth has been driven by the smaller specialty products business, which grew sales by 31% to $164 million, with fire safety sales up 12% to $489 million. The latter category includes airbase retardant, mixing, foam equipment, and ground application units, with specialty products including PCBs, intelligent manufacturing solutions, and others.

So far so good, as margins are difficult to read into, with operating losses on a GAAP basis reported at $201 million. This followed a huge $435 million founder fee, as well as nearly $60 million in amortization charges, as otherwise the business would be very profitable. Adjusted earnings were reported at $206 million, equal to $1.34 per share; these earnings are up twenty-three cents on the year before. However, if we strip out stock-based compensation expenses of around $16 million, that reduces earnings by about ten cents per share.

These expenses also show up on the balance sheet of the firm. Outside a $343 million net debt load, the balance sheet reveals a $536 million founder fee payable and a $117 million preferred stock liability.

Some Perspective

Perimeter went public in 2021 through a popular SPAC vehicle. Initially a $10 stock, momentum was seen after a period of stagnation, as shares have risen to levels in the mid-$20s. While the numbers are a bit distorted, the underlying business is both showing very solid growth and, moreover, very fat margins.

However, all this is a bit shortsighted, as results are impacted by a volatile and sizable advisory fee, showing up both in the profit and loss statement as well as the balance sheet. This actually clouds some underlying performance, which has been very decent, certainly as wildfires have been very prevalent in recent years.

At the start of the year, about 150 million shares were valued at $27, for a $4.1 billion equity valuation that has dropped to about $3.8 billion. However, factoring in net debt, founder fees, and preferred stock liability, enterprise valuations come in closer to $4.8 billion. That, however, compares to a $332 million adjusted EBITDA number reported for 2025, for a 14-15 times multiple on that front, a reasonable multiple even if I do not agree with all the adjustments being made.

Some of this was to change as the company announced a $685 million cash deal to acquire Medical Manufacturing Technologies in December of last year. Also known as MMT, that deal was set to contribute $140 million in sales and $50 million in EBITDA. Valued at 5 times sales and about 14 times EBITDA, multiples were not necessarily very cheap, but reasonable.

While the company claimed a near 3 times leverage ratio, I pegged that higher given the founder liability and preferred stock outstanding. In the end, the capital structure was too complicated for me, given the volatile founders advisory agreement, including the issuance of 1.5% of stock until year-end 2027, with a (potential) sizable variable component lasting until 2031.

And Now?

The founders advisory agreement continues to cause real overhang. In the fourth quarter earnings release, the company indicated that it expects to issue some 11.5 million shares under this agreement in the first quarter, while it confirmed an $82 million cash payment as well.

Needless to say, 11.5 million shares are issued at levels in the mid-twenties, that alone representing a near $300 million handout, such handouts being really dilutive and lasting for years to come. The annual report showed a $435 million variable annual advisory expense after shares rose from a level around the $13 mark to the $28 mark last year, with such expenses seen around 18% of the increase in the market valuation.

On a more positive note, the MMT deal closed in January, as the company indicated on the conference call that it expects meaningful growth in sales and EBITDA for that business going forward. In fact, the company explicitly stated that it is looking at other acquisition opportunities, not industry-specific, but strategy-specific. That means that Perimeter is focusing on quality businesses that focus on dominant market positions in niche segments.

While there is much to like about the performance of the business, the overhang of the advisory agreement makes me cautious, even as shares have come in a little. Given the complicated balance sheet and, moreover, continued payments under the advisory agreement, which are difficult to quantify, I am being very cautious here but still intrigued.

data.title: Genco Shipping: Freight Rates Offer Hope, But The Cycle Isn't On Your Side
data.description:
Introduction

Genco Shipping (GNK) buys and leases ships to transport commodities like iron ore, coal, and grains. The business model of Genco is very straightforward. It earns daily freight through vessel charters. But the business drivers of the company are not that simple because the business performance is based on daily freight through vessel charters.

Genco’s operational cost is mostly fixed, which gives its financials a high operating leverage to freight movements.

As the industry transitions from a supercycle to a more normalized environment, Genco stands at a critical point where macro trends, fleet expansion, and rate dynamics will determine its near term earnings trajectory and investors’ returns.

What is Genco Shipping?

Genco Shipping's business is transporting commodities using ships. Basically, its business model is owning and leasing ships to earn daily freight through charters. So, its revenue drivers are Charter Rates and fleet utilization. The company transports iron ore, coal, grains, and steel across global routes. If we observe the business drivers and end market products, it's easy to conclude that the business is highly cyclical and sensitive to freight rates and demand for end market products.

Currently, Genco Shipping’s fleet portfolio has about 45 vessels. Roughly 40% of the vessel portfolio comprises Capesize/Newcastlemax, which are big ships and transport iron ore, coal, bauxite, etc. These ships are optimized for bulk commodities. The company has labeled this business as a major bulk segment. All commodities that are transported in this segment are transported in huge volumes but generate low value per ton.

The remaining 60% of the fleet portfolio comprises Ultramax/Supramax ships. These vessels are smaller compared to the major bulk segment’s vessels. All these vessels transport fertilizer, grains, sugar, and steel products. These ships are operated under the minor bulk segment. Looking at the end market product, it’s evident that this segment is relatively more stable than others.

About 50% of Genco Shipping's fleets are tied to spot/index-linked markets. So, in a rising freight cycle, the company will have an advantage because it can reprice at higher rates and earn higher income. But the same will be a disadvantage in a declining freight cycle, because the company will have to reprice at lower rates, which can decrease its earnings quickly.

The company prefers to operate in a low-leverage environment, resulting in a low cash breakeven. And the main capital allocation strategy is simple. It focuses on paying regular dividends and buying ships in a weak market.

Overall, the company is heavily linked to the freight rate and demands of the end market products. So, any small change in freight rates or demands of the end market products can have a substantial impact on the company’s earnings. But its capital allocation and low leverage make it a controlled play on the global dry bulk cycle.

Financials

Genco Shipping’s revenue growth in the last five years mostly followed a similar pattern to that of other shipping companies. The revenue growth over the period was not in a straight line. The growth cycle was closely tied to the freight rate cycle and demand cycle of the end market product. The low supply of vessels and the Red Sea crisis were the main reasons behind the supercycle of the freight rates. The strong demand for iron ore and coal was the main cause behind the demand growth of the end market product.

Low supply and high demand for vessels created a growth base for the freight rates. But once the supply and demand started normalizing, revenue started coming down. Overall, revenue growth was volatile and dependent on the freight rate cycle.

Genco’s margins followed the same story. But the impact of changes in freight rates was amplified on margins. The reason behind it is that the company’s business model has solid operating leverage. In simple words, the company’s operating costs are mostly fixed, so when the freight rate increases, revenue starts increasing, but most of the costs remain the same. As a result, we saw amplified EBITDA growth compared to revenue growth.

However, if we look at historical financials, this advantage has turned into a disadvantage in the declining cycle of freight rates because even though revenue declined with the decline in freight rates, most of the costs remained the same.

Genco Shipping has strengthened its balance sheet substantially by repaying significant debt. However, once the freight rates started declining, the debt started rising. The company did not raise debt due to operational distress but to fund growth opportunities, such as fleet acquisition. Even though the reason behind the debt rise looks reasonable, the debt-to-EBITDA ratio has become substantially high. The debt-to-EBITDA ratio can be linked to both a huge increase in debt and a huge decline in EBITDA.

The company’s cash flow conversion also followed the same pattern as the freight cycle. In FY2025, the FCF/Net income turned negative because of the huge capital expenditure and declining CFO caused by weak freight rates.

Overall, Genco Shipping’s financial performance is closely linked to the freight cycle, just like any other shipping company. Currently, the company’s financials are showing that the freight rate supercycle is behind us. Even though the company has decreased the debt significantly since 2020, we have to keep track of the balance sheet health due to a huge increase in the debt/EBITDA ratio. Because it can increase the financial stress in the near term.

With my financial scorecard, the company’s score is 19 out of 25, which puts the company in the ‘above average’ category. But I have adjusted the overall score for cyclicality because most of the financial metrics are volatile due to the impact of cycles. After adjusting for cyclicality, the company falls into the ‘average’ financials category.

What will happen next?

As I have mentioned in my Costmare article, global trade is expected to slow down. In FY2025, the global trade growth is expected to be about 2.6%. The reason behind the slow growth is that global demand is normalizing. This slowdown is also highlighted in the International Monetary Fund’s outlook for FY2026.

However, the macro conditions and outlook explained in my Costamare article can’t be applied directly to Genco Shipping because Costamare is a container shipping business and it is dependent on consumer spending and global trade flows, while Genco Shipping’s business is dry bulk shipping, and it is dependent on the commodity cycle and industrial activity.

The company deals mainly with iron ore, coal, and agricultural trades. Iron ore demand is expected to stay stable, and growth is expected to be flat. China is one of the biggest consumers of iron ore. The weakness in China’s property sector is the main reason behind the slowdown in global iron ore consumption. However, infrastructure and manufacturing activities in China and rising demand from India are keeping the demand stable.

Global coal demand is stable, but in China and India, domestic production is rising. As a result, seaborne trade is expected to decline in the near to medium term. So, we can conclude that the coal outlook is not favorable for Genco Shipping.

Currently, the world is going through geopolitical disruptions in the Middle East and the Red Sea. It has increased the voyage time because ships are forced to take a longer route, which has increased the pressure on the availability. At the same time, Capesize fleet growth is estimated to be lower. So, the geopolitical situation and fleet growth indicate that in the near term, the TCE charter rate can grow because we can see an imbalance in the supply and demand of ships. To be safe, I am assuming 2% flat growth of charter rates.

I am starting with the base revenue of FY2025 after bearing in mind the macro outlook for the near term. Because the imbalance of supply and demand of ships and growth in charter rates support the assumption that the revenue of FY2026 can be greater than that of FY2025.

The active fleet portfolio of the company is expected to scale in FY2026 because 2 Newcastlemax vessels are expected to be delivered, which shows the total portfolio can grow by almost 4.6% compared to the existing portfolio of 45 ships.

On average, 42 fleets were active in FY2025, and to keep the TCE revenue calculation fair, I am assuming that FY2026’s average active fleet size will be 44. And due to geopolitical disruption, the fuel cost is expected to increase as oil prices rise. We don’t know the exact commentary from the management about the voyage expense growth rate. So, to get the voyage expense, I am using the 5-year average of the voyage expense to TCE rate ratio, which is showing that the voyage expense can grow at about 4% YoY in FY2026.

The management has mentioned explicitly in a recent conference call that every $1,000 increase in TCE can raise the EBITDA by about $16 million. I am estimating an increase of about $620 in the TCE rate compared to FY2025’s TCE rate. So, it can approximately increase the EBITDA by $10 million compared to FY2025’s EBITDA. With the increased EBITDA, the total forecasted EBITDA would be $71 million, and EBITDAM would be about 21%. If we look at the macro outlook, these margins look fair because they are near EBITDAM of normalized years like FY2023, and it is very evident that the TCE supercycle is behind us, and currently we are in a normalized environment.

Valuation

If we compare the GNK’s TTM EV/EBITDA with the median of all peer companies in the GNK’s sector, then it looks like the company is significantly overvalued. But EV/EBITDA looks expanded because of the depressed EBITDA margin in FY2025.

I have mentioned multiple times in this article that the company is closely linked to the TCE rate cycle and commodity cycle. Currently, the commodity cycle’s outlook is not very positive, but at the same time, because of the geopolitical crisis, TCE rates can increase in the near term. So overall, the macro outlook looks mixed. Overall, we are likely not going to see huge growth in financials in FY2025.

The information about the bad commodity cycle is not new to the market, and still we are seeing that the company’s valuation stayed strong. So, punishing the multiple for this news would not be right. I am not saying that it would continue to trade at the 19x EV/EBITDA multiple, because there would be an improvement in financials. So, even if it continues to trade at the median of its sectoral peers, it justifies a market capitalization of only $853 million, which shows that the stock is about 16% overvalued compared to the current market capitalization of $1.02 billion. But if we adjust it with the 8% dividend yield, it will show that the investor’s total return would be about -8%.

The problem with Genco’s dividend is that the amount of dividends has never been consistent. But still, I am considering the dividend payment in total returns because, irrespective of the share price or the dividend payment amount, the company’s four-year average dividend yield was 9.4%.

Risks

Genco’s business model is fundamentally dependent on the TCE rates and commodity cycle. Currently, we already know that the commodity cycle outlook is weak, but TCE rates can increase because of the geopolitical issues and the low new supply of vessels. I believe a new supply of the vessels in the global market is certain because building a ship requires lots of time, so the probability of any surprise from this factor is very minimal. However, the Middle East’s geopolitical issues are the main factor in the short-term growth in TCE rates. Currently, everyday situations are changing, and uncertainty is increasing. So, if the TCE rate does not grow in the near term, we can see a correction in the valuation multiples of the company. The main reason behind this is that I am estimating that the impact on valuation could be drastic because its exposure to the spot rate in FY2026 is going to be near 80%.

Conclusion

Putting everything together, the story looks a bit mixed. It can even look confusing at first glance. On one side, freight rates can still see some upside in the near term, mainly because of geopolitical disruptions and limited vessel supply.

But at the same time, the commodity cycle is clearly not strong right now; demand is weak, and we already discussed all those reasons earlier. The thing is, the market already knows this; it’s not a new surprise, and still valuation multiples haven’t really cracked.

Most of the costs are fixed. So, even small moves in freight rates can push earnings up or down quite sharply. The dividend has been pretty consistent over the last few years. So even if price returns don’t look great in the short term, holding for the dividend yield can still make sense if the stock is freshly bought at the current stock price. But the price upside probably comes only when the cycle turns, and honestly, that looks more like a long-term story than a near-term one. So, only investors who are ready to stay invested for the long term should hold this stock for the dividend yield.

data.title: Transocean: Rising Day Rates And Cash Flows Mean I'm Getting On Board
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Offshore drilling is a niche industry I find very interesting to cover. It’s highly volatile, and as of recently there has been some pretty considerable news coming out. I’m referring to the merger between Transocean Ltd. (RIG), the topic of today's article and Valaris Limited (VAL), which happens to be a company I’ve covered before. Since I wrote about VAL on February 4, the stock has risen 65%. To say that it was good timing would be an understatement.

But I thought, rather than writing about it again, it might be more interesting to cover the other part, namely Transocean.

For anyone who's followed my work on the site knows that I’m generally quite bullish on energy as a whole. The thesis with offshore as I see it is that onshore opportunities are drying up and there is a structural shortage of active offshore rigs right now. Demand for oil is there, so companies are forced to look elsewhere for drilling ventures. This is where offshore comes in. Because of this I see rates continuing to climb which widens the gap and raises profits for companies like RIG. Combined with VAL it will be a behemoth, as a standalone investment it’s very strong as well. Rating the stock a Strong Buy.

Operational Overview - Recent Figures

The important distinction with RIG and VAL is that they don’t themselves drill for oil. Rather, they provide the drill ships for companies like Chevron (CVX) or Exxon Mobil (XOM). These rigs get leased with the crews manning them. They drill the hole, secure it, and then let the leasing company come in and extract the oil. That’s the business model of it all. The revenues and profits come from the gap between day rates and operating expenses. Similar to a crack spread for an oil refinery. Operating costs often stay the same, but demand and new contracts determine the top line or the day rates.

For RIG the average daily revenues have been on a steady climb over the past few years, rising 19.4%, or 9.7% per year between 2023 and 2025. The offshore drilling equipment market is set to grow steadily to $113 billion by 2030 according to one report. Nearly half of the rigs that RIG has are located in the U.S. Gulf. Last years there were record levels of oil production here which tells the story I think I’m trying to get across, that offshore is ripe for a boom. But another compelling market is Brazil, where RIG also has rigs. Offshore production is set to compound at a steady 3.6% until 2034. Again, supporting my argument that day rates will steadily continue to climb for RIG. Between 2023 and 2030 global oil demand is set to rise 6.3%. Even with more energy coming from renewable sources, this “legacy” source is still ticking along just fine.

In the October fleet report one of the key things I found was just how many of the rigs were operating on priced options, meaning they aren’t locked in and can fluctuate somewhat. In the latest report, from February this has changed largely to “firm” instead which locks in prices. Normally you have slowly rising day rates as seen in the image above. What starts at $397,000 per day grows to $447,000 in a period of about 5 years.

Sure, the difference between Q3 2026 in October and February is small. But look at Q4 2026. It shows a solid 3.6% QoQ growth rate, for harsh environment rigs that is. It comes as customers to Transocean Equinox for example changed from Price Option to Firm. The day rate for the last period stayed the same at $540,000, but more importantly is that it’s now locked in. For reference this contract ends in January 2027.

Global marketed supply of jackups, drillships and semisubs stands at around 439, 89 and 73 units, which is down by five, seven and two units respectively, when compared to the end of 2024

Given how demand is still strong for oil, and the fact global drillship supply is still declining I think we are heading towards a perfect storm of undersupply and rising prices. Looking at the fleet of RIG, there has been no change since October, meaning no new drillships coming into the fleet. This helps keep supply constrained and pushes rates further.

Strong Revenue Outlook With Continued Shareholder Value Creation

Below I've made a table looking at where the revenues might be for this year based on what RIG itself has reported in terms of average day rates. Most of the rigs are now locked in, meaning the main thing that would change revenues is the uptime for both categories.

Our rig utilization rate declines as a result of idle and stacked rigs and during shipyard, contract preparation and mobilization periods.

After a tough few years in the offshore market it seems that it’s finally heading in the right direction as RIG have been reporting consistently rising rig utilization rates.

The forecast gets me to $3.13 billion in total revenues for this year. Again, there is a decent chance that this gets surpassed if utilization rates for the drillships rise more than what I’ve projected. I’ve been deliberately assuming conservative figures for the year. Still, with a market cap of $7.2 billion this puts shares at a FWD P/S of just 2.3x. This sits above the sector median (energy) which is 1.64x. But given the strong upside potential present here and the merger with VAL I think a moderate premium is definitely justified. The main reason why this is below the 2025 revenues has to do with the fleet declining from 31 to 27 this year. I’m also not adding the reimbursables and amortized fees that occur when a rig is “out of service.” Normally this would add about 8 to 12% to the top line. For the sake of this I do prefer looking purely at the day rate revenues.

The operating cash flows for RIG has been climbing at a steady pace the past few years. When compared to the revenues the past few years I see the conversion rate being 18.8%, 12.6% and 10.6%. RIG has been able to raise this on a consistent basis. If this trend keeps up RIG should see a conversion rate close to 24% this year. Based on my revenue projection from before that is $751 million. When adding the fees I mentioned, OCF would instead be in the range of $826 million ($3.13 billion x 10% / 100 * 24).

Because CapEx spending is also dropping, RIG could generate about $703 million in free cash flows in 2026, assuming we’ll see the same spending as last year. This means shares trade at 10.3x 2026 FCF. Again, at a premium to the sector, but actually at a discount to RIG’s historical 5-year figure of 11.91x. The discount is about 14%. So even if the stock has run up 200% the past 12 months, you are still not buying at a great premium necessarily.

So where is the money going? Well, the past 2 years it’s been going towards paying down debt. $3.6 billion has been paid down in just 2 years. Long-term debt is now $5.2 billion, down from $6.1 billion. Cash is $620 million. Shareholder value creation comes from the theory that paying down more debt then they issue RIG is raising its market cap, assuming EV stays the same.

Because enterprise value is market cap + net debt, paying down in theory $500 million in debt and EV stays the same, the market cap has to rise. The difference in 2025 between repayments and new debt was roughly $1 billion. At the start of 2025 the market cap was hovering around $3.5 billion. It’s now over $7 billion. So clearly, paying down debt has unlocked considerable shareholder value.

The Merger - There Still Exists Upside

So, this might be what most who clicked the article were looking forward to, the merger with Valaris. I think it’s important to look at RIG individually first, and I think it looks excellent as is.

As for the merger which was announced in early February, it looks great at surface level. RIG is the one acquiring VAL and the split will be roughly 53% RIG and 47% VAL afterwards in an all-stock transaction valued at $5.8 billion.

The change here is that the fleet composition will look different. VAL is mostly working with jack-ups, something RIG divested from a few years ago. With VAL 31 jack-ups now enter the picture. But then you also have the 50/50 joint venture with Saudi Aramco called ARO Drilling. This adds exposure to the Middle East for RIG, perhaps at a difficult time, but still. But VAL has on top of 31 jack-ups also 13 ultra-deepwater drillships, in the same category as RIG. The integration costs are likely minimal here, it’s nearly a flat revenue hike when the two combine. With 33 UDW it makes RIG one of the largest offshore companies in the industry. Peers like Noble Corp (NE) are now a lesser threat.

Because the deal is all-stock RIG is not issuing more debt, meaning it can continue its trend of paying down its debt pile. As for the value VAL brings apart from the fleet, we have $599 million in cash and only $1 billion in LT-debt. Arguably, VAL is in a much better financial position than RIG.

Risks

The main risk for offshore right now is I think a rapid decline in oil prices. Offshore is more expensive to invest and operate than onshore. If oil prices decline to where they were at the year 2025, hovering around $60 per barrel, signing new contracts with RIG or peers becomes a lot less attractive.

But since the prices have surged the market has shifted and suddenly offshore doesn't look all that bad anymore for most companies. Even if there is a reliable ceasefire and end to the conflict in the Middle East, oil prices will likely remain elevated for longer this and next year.

Unlocks more than $200 million in identified cost synergies, additive to Transocean’s ongoing cost savings initiative

Looking at the merger, there are risks there, mainly about whether or not the $200 million synergy benefits actually materialize or not for the merger company. If not, I expect there to be an added risk premium and a correction in the stock price. That won’t occur until at least 2027 most likely though as more reports come out.

The Full Picture

As a standalone company, I think that RIG is in an excellent position right now. I see more upside as the debt repayments seem to unlock serious shareholder value right now. With the announced merger, RIG will also be one of the largest in its industry and could command premium pricing for its fleet.

Valuation wise it’s not that rich with the P/S sitting at about 2.3x this year's revenues and at 10.3x FCF, assuming CapEx and OCF conversion rates remain the same. Even without the merger news I would be bullish on RIG. Rating shares a Strong Buy.

data.title: March CPI Just Flipped The Inflation Debate
data.description:
The March CPI release is adding fuel to a fragile ceasefire rally.

Headline inflation landed exactly where economists expected (3.2% y/y and 0.9% m/m), but core CPI, the figure that matters more for the Fed's monetary policy, came in softer than expected (2.6% y/y vs. 2.7% y/y consensus).

Despite the Strait of Hormuz still remaining largely closed, with post-ceasefire transits in the low teens or high single-digit figures depending on the source you are reading, I am increasingly more bullish on the broader markets.

In fact, I already loaded the truck yesterday, and I may add even more stocks to my portfolio today.

Why?

Simply because the market is still overly pessimistic about the Fed's monetary policy. Despite the encouraging March CPI report, economists still believe in zero cuts this year.

It is my view that these experts are dead wrong, although I am not singing victory yet.

If April and May CPI prints rhyme with the March report, I see a real chance of the first rate cut as soon as June this year. Below, I explain why.

The Long-Awaited March CPI Report

I’m the first to admit that over the past few weeks, I’ve been checking the Cleveland Fed’s inflation nowcast more than I want to recognize (daily, sometimes, even twice a day as new data came in).

Prior to the BLS March CPI release, the Cleveland Fed nowcast for headline CPI was 0.84% m/m and 3.25% y/y, with core CPI at 0.20% m/m and 2.60% y/y. The Reuters consensus was 0.9% m/m and 3.3% y/y for headline CPI and 0.3% m/m and 2.7% y/y for core CPI.

Well, the actual numbers from the Bureau of Labor Statistics just came in, and it’s a big relief to see them (especially because I loaded up the truck yesterday after putting my cash back to work).

The headline CPI was up 0.9% m/m and 3.3% y/y, while core CPI increased 0.2% m/m and 2.6% y/y.

In other words, the CPI matched the consensus on the headline, but the more important (from an interest rate policy perspective) core CPI came in softer than the consensus anticipated.

As I'm writing, the market's reaction to this read was mostly positive, except for the Dow Jones, as one would expect due to the predominance of non-tech and non-AI growth stock exposure.

From a sector perspective, I see more sectors today in the green than in the red, which is a good sign after such an important economic release.

Coming back to the March CPI, the main focus was on whether energy costs were spilling over into other areas. They didn't, mostly.

Food was flat overall, which is a big relief:

The index for food was unchanged over the month as the index for food away from home rose 0.2 percent, while the index for food at home fell 0.2 percent.

To me, this suggests that the higher fuel prices are largely absorbed by corporations and are not passed on to consumers.

Speaking of fuel prices, gasoline alone accounted for nearly 75% of the monthly increase in the headline CPI. To be specific, energy was up 10.9% on the month, with gasoline up 21.2% and fuel oil up 30.7%.

On the oil side, Brent is still hovering in the mid-$90s level, as the ceasefire looks very fragile and the transits through the Strait of Hormuz appear to be conditional on Iran's approval, with some sources suggesting there are passage fees for tankers.

Trump didn't seem to be happy about this:

By the end of March, the U.S. average retail gasoline price was sitting at $3.99/gallon, a 55% increase from Jan 2025.

Notably, jet fuel was up 84% in the same timeframe. Therefore, I was not surprised to see some elements within the core CPI ticking up in March.

Specifically, airline fares increased by 2.7%, and transportation services rose by 0.6% in March, which suggests that in some pockets of the market, there has been an early oil pass-through to consumers.

That said, I am not sweating about that increase. Why?

Core goods, excluding food and energy, rose just 0.1%. In other words, the fear that corporations are broadly passing through the transportation costs to consumers (outside of a few pockets in the market) is overblown, at least for now.

Am I going to pop a bottle of champagne tonight after this CPI report?

You bet I am, despite the fact that core CPI is still above the Fed's 2% target. The reason I am optimistic is largely that the print didn't show a generalized surge in prices across the basket.

Therefore, if the April/May CPI shows a similar result, likely incoming Fed Chair Kevin Warsh will have one more reason to (aggressively) cut interest rates, on top of the one below:

Bear in mind that Trump wants interest rates as low as 1%. I don't think he will get that any time soon, but directionally, I strongly believe the Fed fund rate will be down this year.

Based on the CME Group survey results below, I believe I am mostly alone with my dovish view, which presents a strong catalyst for the second half of the year.

Back to inflation, there are still more secondary effects to look for in the upcoming months, particularly for items exposed to jet fuel, diesel, freight, fertilizer, plastics, and other transport-sensitive costs.

So I don't think we are out of the woods yet. However, I don't expect new highs for oil prices, and I am increasingly leaning toward the view that the lows in the S&P 500 are behind us.

Bottom Line

I’m now more confident that the broader markets have bottomed, as the Hormuz blockade is not showing signs of significantly driving up core inflation.

As seen above, we are just 2.5% from new highs, which reflects the market confidence through these uncertain times.

First, I say confidence, and not arrogance, because, as we learned last year with Trump's tariffs, things are always less impactful than what the headlines and "experts" say.

Second, I say uncertain because, from what I'm reading, there have been no oil tankers traversing the Strait of Hormuz. It appears that the only post-ceasefire passages were done by dry bulk and container ships, with numbers in the high single digits to low teens. That's an improvement, but still below the 138 per day before the conflict began.

As Sultan Al Jaber, chief executive officer of Abu Dhabi National Oil Co., puts it, the Strait of Hormuz is still not open.

That said, the market is looking past the noise as Vice President JD Vance heads to Islamabad. I would keep a close eye on what happens this Sunday in Pakistan, as I believe the end of the conflict may be in sight.

data.title: Atmus Filtration Technologies' Surge Isn't A Good Reason To Turn Optimistic
data.description:
Back in September of last year, I decided to rate Atmus Filtration Technologies Inc. (ATMU) a "Hold." I acknowledged that the business was a solid operator. But I was not enthusiastic about the price. Despite continued growth on the top and bottom lines, the stock looked priced at levels that were more in the fair value range than in the undervalued range. The market has since disagreed with my assessment, pushing shares up 29.9% while the S&P 500 is down 0.5%. This return disparity is great for its investors but clearly a challenge to my thesis. So as part of my analysis, I decided to dig deeper again and see whether my assessment should still be viewed as credible. Although the company is certainly trading on the cheaper end of the spectrum compared to other similar firms, it is priced at levels that are difficult to justify if your goal is to describe it as undervalued. So at the risk that the market might continue to move against my call, I believe that maintaining it as a "Hold" makes sense here.

Checking In On Atmus Filtration Technologies

If you are new to Atmus Filtration Technologies, it would be helpful to understand what the company is and what it does. At its core, the company operates as a player in the filtration products space. It caters to on-highway commercial vehicles, off-highway agricultural vehicles, construction equipment, mining equipment, power generation vehicles, and more. To many investors, this might not seem all that exciting a space to play in. But for me, it is intriguing. This is especially true when you consider that the business has a couple of things going forward.

Although it is a U.S.-centric enterprise, with revenue from the United States and Canada accounting for 58% of sales last year, it gets large chunks of revenue from Latin America, Europe, the Middle East, Africa, and the Asia Pacific region of the world. The other thing going for it is that it is not really exposed all that much to the OEM space. Instead, it has positioned itself as a player in the aftermarket. About 87% of sales come from that space. That is appealing to me on its own because the aftermarket industry has a tendency to be less susceptible to broader economic challenges.

The newest data that investors have right now covers through the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company was $446.6 million. That was up soundly from the $406.7 million that the company reported a year earlier. Management attributed this 9.8% increase to a mixture of things, including higher prices, higher volumes, and favorable foreign currency fluctuations. Higher prices added about 5% to the top line for the company. Higher volumes added 4%. And foreign currency fluctuations aided to the tune of 1%. Management did provide additional details about this and their latest earnings release. According to management, one contributor to the upside that the business saw was the firm's launch of its NanoNet N3 filtration media, which won a very prestigious award in the industry. On top of this, the firm is continuing to push into the aftermarket space since it views that as a cornerstone of its growth potential.

As revenue increased, profitability improved also. Net income expanded from $40.1 million to $48 million. It is worth noting that certain manufacturing costs decreased for the company, which worked well when paired with the higher prices and increased volumes. Other profitability metrics grew similarly. Operating cash flow, for starters, more than doubled from $20 million to $47.5 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a surge from $43.7 million to $83.6 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $77.7 million to $85.2 million.

The chart above shows financial performance for 2025 in its entirety compared to 2024. Thanks to $51.9 million of increased volumes and $50 million of higher prices, both of which eclipsed the $7.3 million negative impact associated with foreign currency fluctuations, revenue shot up from $1.67 billion to $1.76 billion. Not surprisingly, overall profitability expanded also. In fact, as the company's gross profit margin grew from 27.7% to 28.2%, even at a time when tariffs cost the business $36.2 million directly, overall net income expanded from $185.6 million to $207.4 million. Other profitability metrics rose as well, as the aforementioned chart illustrates. In fact, there's not a single profitability metric that I looked at that showed the business worsening.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year, management has provided some interesting guidance. They expect revenue to come in at between $1.945 billion and $2.015 billion. At the midpoint, this would be 12.2% above the $1.76 billion that the company reported last year. However, it would be a mistake to overstate how much of this growth will be organic. You see, back in November of last year, management announced that the company was acquiring Koch Filter Corporation, a producer of essential air filtration systems for certain markets like the industrial and commercial HVAC space, data centers, power generation, and other related industries. Despite the business bringing in annual revenue of only $156 million, management ended up paying $450 million in cash for the enterprise.

This works out to an EV-to-EBITDA multiple of about 13.9. But when you factor in the present value of expected tax benefits from the transaction, which alone reduces the price of the acquisition to $395 million on a net basis, and you factor in cost synergies that management is forecasting, the multiple lowers to 10.9. In a moment, you will see that this is actually quite low considering how shares of Atmus Filtration Technologies are trading at the moment. That deal ultimately closed in early January. And guidance for 2026 does incorporate it. However, what does complicate matters is the fact that we need to factor in, when evaluating the company on a comparable basis, what owning Koch Filter Corporation would have meant for the enterprise for 2025.

In the table above, you can see, factoring in the data that management provided as well as reasonable estimates for other items, how much in EBITDA, net profits, and operating cash flow Koch Filter Corporation should bring to Atmus Filtration Technologies on an annual basis. I used the same tax rate that the company had for 2025. For depreciation, depletion, and amortization, I assumed that the amount would equal the same percent of revenue that Atmus Filtration Technologies had for last year. And for the interest rate on the $395 million in net debt, I used a reading of 5.2% to reflect the current credit facility debt that is paying based on SOFR plus a range of between 1.25% and 1.75% on top of that.

Using the adjusted figures for 2025, then, we can see in the chart above how shares of Atmus Filtration Technologies are priced at the moment. These certainly aren't bad multiples. But I wouldn't say that they put the company in the cheap category either. Relative to other similar firms, of course, the stock is certainly on the cheaper end of the spectrum. And that gives me a good reason to appreciate the stance that those who are bullish about the business might have on it. But as somebody who prioritizes absolute value as opposed to relative value, I put more weight on the absolute value side of the equation.

Takeaway

Based on the data that's available right now, it is my belief that the market has been a little overly optimistic regarding Atmus Filtration Technologies. The company is certainly a great firm. And it is cheaper compared to some other similar enterprises. But on the whole, the stock is not cheap enough to justify a bullish outlook in my view. So for now, I am maintaining it as a "Hold" candidate.

data.title: The Magnificent 7 Broke - That's Exactly Why I'm Buying
data.description:
Let me give you a quick image: a rock star arriving late on stage, everyone waiting for her, everyone knows her, everyone continues to listen and watch, but they can no longer ignite the same enthusiasm as before. That's how I see the Magnificent Seven, who seem to have gone from being untouchable, as I often say, to mere mortals. Software was marketed with the impression that AI would destroy their business model overnight. Each additional $1 billion dollar added into CapEx was viewed by investors as more of a risk than an investment.

Obviously, the war provided the perfect catalyst for correction and increased uncertainty in the markets. As a result, it made everything more violent. However, according to me, the primary issue was never the War. The issue was always Valuation. As stated previously in other articles on Seeking Alpha, the market refused to fall, despite stretched multiples and a less-than-reassuring news flow. Possibly it was retail support, possibly it was typical Inertia associated with major trends, or possibly there were other forces at work. What is evident is that the markets needed to take a breath. Finally, the market took a breath.

The real question is not whether sentiment has decreased further. Clearly that has happened. The real question is whether this fall has already created a good buying opportunity or if it is too soon to put money back into the industry.

For myself, I had already unloaded some of my tech exposure some time ago, and those who follow my analyses know this. Therefore, today the issue is not chasing/rushing to get back into this area, but rather identifying whether we are currently experiencing a normal phase of valuation decompression or if it signifies a more critical event. Since these periods of time (when markets move from euphoria to rejection) typically provide the most favorable opportunities, those who can differentiate between fear and a deterioration in fundamental values will be able to capitalize on them.

Tech's Weaknesses

Tech has shown unusual dynamics so far in 2026. Since the beginning of the year, the seven “Magnificent” have underperformed versus the overall market. The S&P 500 (SP500) index has traded relatively flat.

However, rather than viewing this performance as a mass decline, I view this as a dispersion within the group. While some companies have experienced significant declines, other companies are performing well. These two different areas will result in a very different perspective going forward. One could say investors are exiting the space. In my opinion, a better way to describe what is occurring is that investors are simply rethinking their expectations regarding what they expect from the space.

For several years prior to 2026, the tech space was essentially viewed as an asset-light area. High-margin growth, low incremental investments (i.e., capital expenditures), and good conversion of revenue to cash were all expected from tech businesses. The rise of artificial intelligence changed that expectation. Today, many large-cap companies in the space are forced to purchase, finance or construct production capacity such as servers, chips, data centers and/or energy. When an entity increases its capital intensity, the market views it through a completely new lens.

#1 CapEx

One of the major issues related to increasing capital intensity is free cash flow. Increasing capital expenditures (CAPEX) decreases free cash availability for near-term use by a company for dividend payments, buybacks and/or acquisitions. Additionally, increased CapEx increases a company’s risk associated with execution because only adequate future returns justify the current level of investment. Therefore, the market is switching from a “growth-at-all-costs” mentality to a far more conservative approach – i.e., measuring return on invested capital.

This change in focus creates great interest in how these companies are being valued today. Not because the AI thesis failed nor because innovation ended, but because virtually every segment of the tech space is being priced with a great deal more skepticism. It is when the market moves away from chasing a story and focuses on actual numbers that the best opportunities tend to surface.

#2 Duration

A second major concern facing the sector is duration. Because growth stocks are viewed as long-duration instruments, those sectors experience greater pressure during periods of rising inflation and/or higher real yields. Thus, geopolitical events matter significantly. For example, if an event such as a block of Hormuz occurs or an oil price shock results from a major conflict and/or disruption in supply, certain segments of the tech space tend to be negatively impacted due to perceived inflationary pressures. Conversely, should fear of recession prevail and real interest rates begin to drop, many of the same tech stocks which suffered losses during previous periods of fear may rapidly gain favor.

#3 Monetization

Thirdly, and equally importantly, there has been a credibility crisis on monetization. As late as 2024, simply mentioning "AI" would result in many stocks getting revalued. That’s now not sufficient for 2026. Markets want figures, not slogans; they want to know about unit economies, backlogs, pricing power, run-rates, and number of customers. Essentially, markets do not want to be told how great the future will be. They want to begin measuring the present.

#4 Dispersion

Lastly, there is a rotation within the sector itself. Perhaps the most interesting aspect of this story is that tech is not moving as one singular entity. Underneath, there is considerable dispersion amongst sub-sectors, with frequent rotations occurring amongst them. In certain times during this cycle, software will be selected for targeting due to its perception of vulnerability, replaceability, or exposure to margin compression. At the same time, infrastructure and semiconductor firms are reaping benefits as they are perceived as the ones "laying down the AI rails," not just trying to find ways to make money from it later on.

It may seem overly simplistic when considering the market is separating those that "create rails" from those that "sell tickets." However, such distinctions are critical at this time.

Therefore, the only seemingly obvious question is: Is this weakness an an ordinary repricing of assets (and thus a normal response to overvaluation), or does this represent the notion that AI will ultimately become so expensive relative to possible future earnings? I have formed my own opinion regarding this issue and will detail why below in this article.

AI Capex: Cost Today, Barrier Tomorrow

Bloomberg Intelligence estimates that the total capital expenditures for six major U.S. hyperscalers are expected to exceed $750 billion dollars in 2026, representing a +80% YoY increase after another record setting year in 2025. As shown below, the data illustrates the jump in scale and the trend of the costs being "stuck" at a high level in future years.

Investors may respond to such information in one of two fashions:

  1.
